channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Binyamin Netanyahu is down—but not out
item.description: Ahead of an election due in October, a divided opposition and the war could hand him a lifeline

item.title: Trump Claims ‘Great Progress’ in Iran Peace Talks
item.description: At the same time, the White House has threatened to obliterate Tehran’s energy sector if a deal isn’t reached soon.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Will Trump send troops into Iran?
item.description: The president must be aware that the political costs of a land war could be “extremely high”, writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: The Houthis Are Now in the War—But How Deep?
item.description: Iran’s Yemeni proxy group could make a bad energy market catastrophic if it targets the Red Sea.

item.title: For China’s officials, the goal was once growth. Now it’s loyalty
item.description: An obsession with GDP is replaced by an obsession with Xi Jinping

item.title: Iran War Shatters South Asians’ Gulf Dreams
item.description: Migrants are seeing flights and jobs vanish.

item.title: Why a startup is teaching human brain cells to play “Doom”
item.description: They could herald a new type of computing

item.title: After Iran, gold is looking less glittery
item.description: Is the yellow metal the new crypto?

item.title: The Scars in Africa-Europe Relations
item.description: Relations between Africa and Europe continue to be shaped – and distorted – by the historical trauma of five centuries of slavery and colonialism. Donald Trump’s return to the White House has exposed European leaders’ lack of confidence and moral fiber, as well as their unwillingness to treat Africa as an equal.

item.title: The Unruly Few: Mongolia Faces Another Government Change
item.description: In just 10 months, Mongolia has now weathered three changes of governments, highlighting the lack of political stability.

item.title: Trump Is Losing the War in Iran
item.description: One month in, the Islamic Republic is winning merely by surviving.

item.title: South Korean Unification Minister Uses North Korea’s Formal Name
item.description: Seoul's use of Pyongyang's formal state title signals a historic policy shift, but the Lee administration's two-state vision is constrained by the constitution, Washington's nuclear red lines, and its own unification mandate.

item.title: Donald Trump and the art of bad diplomacy
item.description: One of the advisers the president ignores is his younger self

item.title: Putin’s Persian Problem
item.description: Don’t overthink it. The Iran war is bad for Russia.

item.title: America Should Beware of Economic Hubris
item.description: Even if the US economy continues outperforming its peers, it will not necessarily remain insulated from the Iran war’s adverse spillovers. Already, higher energy and borrowing costs are exacerbating the affordability pressures many Americans face, creating downside risks for jobs, consumption, and growth.

item.title: How the Iran War Is Reshaping Kazakhstan’s Role in Eurasia
item.description: With both northern routes through Russia and southern routes through Iran facing disruption or heightened political risk, the Middle Corridor is moving from an alternative option to a strategic necessity.

item.title: Alatau, Kazakhstan’s Futuristic Crypto City, Faces Ground-Level Realities
item.description: While officials promote Alatau as a model for digital innovation, existing residents continue to face basic infrastructure challenges.

item.title: The Venezuela Donald Trump “runs” is a land of surreal contrasts
item.description: Animal spirits are stirring. The opposition has hope. The regime’s softening of repression is uneven

item.title: The Iran War Has Escaped Its Authors
item.description: Escalation is already shattering Washington’s illusion of control.

item.title: A Coordinated Trans-Eurasian Threat: The Deepening China-Russia Strategic Partnership
item.description: Sino-Russian cooperation and antagonism to the Western-led order is hardly new, but their partnership has reached new depths.

item.title: Remaking Europe’s Energy System for the Age of AI
item.description: Europe will never have the abundant fossil-fuel resources of the US, but it can still achieve the kinds of energy diversification and cost reductions seen in China. This would protect Europe from price spikes and enable the EU to compete in the defining economic race of our time: turning electricity into intelligence.

item.title: Is South Korea About to Finally Get Full Control of Its Own Military?
item.description: OPCON transition would have major implications not just for the Korean Peninsula, but for the broader geopolitical struggle in the Indo-Pacific.

item.title: CANZUK: A Fringe Idea Whose Time Has Come?
item.description: In a more volatile world, closer political, economic and strategic partnership between Canada, Australia, New Zealand and the United Kingdom could benefit all.

item.title: Despite Growing Crackdown, Independent Religious Groups Defy China’s Communist Party
item.description: The latest round of repression shows the risks underground churches face – and how Chinese people continue to seek faith outside state control.

item.title: Why Iran’s Escalation Strategy Is Likely to Backfire
item.description: Iran’s current strategy in the war with the United States and Israel is designed to demonstrate the regime’s resilience, and to show that the region will not know peace unless Iran does. But the economic and diplomatic consequences ultimately will weigh more heavily on Iran than on its adversaries.

item.title: The Sad Demise of China’s Economic Debate
item.description: China has never been an open society where ideas can be debated freely, save for one area: economic policy. But such engagement is no longer possible, reflected in the demise of the China Development Forum, which has morphed from a platform of true give-and-take into one cheerleading session after another.

item.title: Taking the Battle for Human Attention Seriously
item.description: Now that Meta and YouTube have been held liable by a US jury for deliberately addicting young users, leading to numerous mental health problems, it is time to stop viewing human attention as an exploitable resource. In fact, it is a collective infrastructure essential to the survival of open societies.

item.title: NCP Leader Nasiruddin Patwary on Electoral Engineering and Constitutional Reform in Bangladesh
item.description: “In our view, Sheikh Hasina surrendered too much space to India, and the BNP government now appears to be moving in a similar direction.”

item.title: ‘Unconditional Surrender’ Is Always an Illusion
item.description: An American myth reemerges for the Iran war.

item.title: In Photos: Life along Myanmar’s Border
item.description: Refugees scrounge out a life on both sides of the Moei River bordering Thailand.

item.title: Wartime Budgets Are an X-Ray of Presidential Priorities
item.description: By choosing escalation in Vietnam, Lyndon Johnson’s plan to fund both guns and butter unraveled.

item.title: What the Energy Emergency Means for Philippine Politics
item.description: The Marcos administration faces the daunting task of preserving calm and maintaining public support amid the ongoing oil supply crisis.

item.title: How Congress Can Prevent a Quagmire in Iran
item.description: Authorize limited airstrikes but rule out ground forces.

item.title: What the Iran War Means for China
item.description: Beijing fears American volatility more than American power.

item.title: Trump Has Got Europe All Wrong
item.description: Why America needs the European Union.

item.title: ASEAN Summit to Proceed in May, but Preparatory Meetings Will Be Shifted Online
item.description: The economic ramifications of the war in Iran are likely to dominate the May 8-9 ASEAN Leaders' Summit in Cebu.

item.title: New Governments in Bangladesh and Nepal Open Window for India to Recast Ties with Neighbors
item.description: In building ties with these countries, India will need to factor in China.

item.title: The Rise and Fall of the Persianate World in Central and South Asia
item.description: Persian spread as it was a prestigious and developed language that worked as a lingua franca for trade and administration across much of the eastern Islamic world.

item.title: Thailand Brokers Deal With Iran Allowing Vessels to Pass Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The arrangement with Tehran is likely to alleviate some of the worst impacts of the fuel shortages that threaten further to constrain the country's economy.

item.title: An Upgraded ATIGA and the Impact of the Collapse of Thailand Plus One
item.description: Was the single market and production base a fantasy?
